speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling us about your sister Judith.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was running after her. About halfway to Lida, she told me the whole story and she gave me a name, where to contact in Voranava-- Polish guy. I came-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I came the same night to Voranava, because I couldn't get back to Radun, because they, they warned me they don't want to see me anymore in Radun. No question about to register me, because they got to register me on a false name that I am not Radun resident. Otherwise, no Jew is allowed to, to take me in and to [INAUDIBLE] over sleep.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got an opportunity to go to Radun-- and I have an address and a name-- to a Polish man. He was in the underground, and probably Judy got a connection with him. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I came-- when I went to Voranava-- same night, they took her away afternoon-- it was afternoon, a Sunday afternoon. They took her away to Lida, and I set on my way to Voranava.

speaker: interviewee
text: The way was the only lucky thing with me. I've got a very good sense of directions. And it wasn't any problem to get to the point where I point out myself, not to ask. Because the minute I ask somebody, I discover myself. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- but it was hard to find my-- the street where he is, and it was at night. So I got in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the first house with not knowing this is a Jewish-- people live here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knock on the window. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And, who is there? So I say, ah, a Jew. I saw already, it was Jewish people. They had the-- they asked me, I saw him. And I find out that is a relative of my father-- not a cousin, a second or a third cousin, but they know each other. I didn't know him. He opens me the door, and I say, I'm [NON-ENGLISH]. What is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Livitowsky. His name was Livitowsky, Jesl Livitowsky. So I say, I am [NON-ENGLISH], son of Manny. And I tell them a short-- this story. I run away from the massacre. I met Judy. They took her to Lida. In Lida, gave me an address to a Polack. I gave the name. I gave them [INAUDIBLE]. He say, OK. But you cannot go there at night, because it's the other side of the town, and they'll catch you. Don't go there. I'll show you tomorrow, next day. When I came in, I told them all the story. You know, they gave me whatever, a bite or a drink. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it came out that his son-- he lives in Israel now-- was on the same list that Judy in Ejszyszki, he was on the list in Voranava. He was participating with the Russians while they were occupated there. And he was also in hiding. But he was hiding in the house in a double wall. But he never came out to me. He told me after, when I saw him in Israel a  dozen years later. Anyway, they gave me a place to sleep. Next day, he told me-- he took me to the-- first of all, to the Judenrat, because the Judenrat there was a, a, a resident from Ejszyszki. Incidentally, for the last couple of years before the occupation, he moved over to Voranava and he became a Judenrat of the August in Voranava. When I tell him the story also about Judy-- they took-- and he has very good connections with the SS in Voranava. And he says, don't go to this peasant, to these Polacks who Judy, Judy gave me, because he is-- he's watched 24 hours from the SS. Whoever goes in, in his apartment, in this neighborhood, in this surrounding-- they'll arrest him, the SS. He was a big shot in the Communist Party before.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, but I would like to know the fate of Judith. He said, don't worry. I'll tell you. Give me a couple days. I'll tell you. He got very good connections with the SS. How did he have connections with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, [YIDDISH]. Drinking. I don't know what else. Money. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I say, stay here a couple days. I'll tell you exactly what happened to Judith. I went back to Jesl Livitowsky at home. We stayed. I sleep over the night. Meanwhile, the wound got healed. I came back to myself. He changed me my clothes that Judith gave me, from like, like a shepherd clothes, you know, which doesn't fit on me. And it's, it's, it's-- you can see it's, it's, it's, it's not a normal person. He gave me his son's clothes, who was in hiding but he never discover himself that I am-- he is there. And after I talk, we sleep over, I eat. Fall asleep at night. It was a Sunday night. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They knocked in door. Somebody knocked in the door. They opened it up. Three Germans coming in. They're searching if somebody here from Ejszyszki or from Lithuanian, sleeping or remaining here when they are not registered as Voranava residents. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was sleeping, and I hear somebody slapping me for the foot. I sit up. While I'm sitting up, feel with a stick over my head. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Wake up.

speaker: interviewee
text: 'Documents.' I have not documents. 'Where are you?' I was half asleep and and, and, and confused already from the-- from the [INAUDIBLE] head. You know, when a sleeping man comes up from sleep and suddenly you got a stick over your head, fast and hard. I say, I'm from Ejszyszki. 'Dress up and come.'

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened that night, from Ejszyszki, a round-up waked up 100 people from the massacre, and they settled in Radun or in Voranava. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now they came to Radun and Voranava. They were about 7, 10 kilometers from Ejszyszki, 13 kilometers from Ejszyszki. And they took back together all the refugees from Ejszyszki. They took 100 people, refugees from Voranava. In Radun, they were successful. There was the action in the same night in Radun, too. But in Radun, they were successful because they act better officially than the Voranava Judenrat. Everybody got registered as a Radun-- residents. In Voranava they didn't do that. I don't know why-- because they were too sure with themselves, or whether because Hirsch [? Koppelman, ?] the head of Judenrat, had good connections with the Ju-- with the SS. And they took-- they put together 100 people from all over. And they took me, dressed up, from house to house, I go with them two going in the next house. One stays with me and is watching me. They put together about four or five-- four people, Jews who ran away from Ejszyszki or from Vilna, and they put it in Kommandatur-- from their Kommandatur. They [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] concentrated the all people who they catched who were not from Voranava in a movies-- in a-- in a movie hall, a cinema.

speaker: interviewee
text: But on the way, they took us in into their Kommandatur. And it wasn't a SS unit. It was a communication unit, you know? Translation, radio, because I saw on the-- on the signs of their-- of the cloth. But from sadism, they helped the SS on that night in this action. And from sadism on their own, they put in whoever they catch-- before they putting them in in the cinema, with, with the second-- with the-- with the rest of the catch, they put in in their headquarters. And they start to beat us.

speaker: interviewee
text: They catch one guy from Ejszyszki, Teivele Blacher, who is also on the picture there in the [YIDDISH] with me. And they undressed us. And they put such a big-- a small table in the middle, such a little area table. And they undressed us again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they saw I'm still, I'm still wounded. And they started to ask me, Where are the scratches? Where is the wound under here? I got still the wound on this little patch from [INAUDIBLE]. Ah it happened in Ejszyszki. Have to hide from them. So I am in the soup. I can-- can I save myself?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they undressed us and put us on the-- on a wall. And one by one they're putting on the bent-- the bench. And they start to beat with sticks, with whips, whatever everybody can catch. Four people, 1-2-3-4, 1-2-3-4. They start beating everybody like. And the skin became like a pipe, all colors.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were the Germans that were doing the beating?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans. They took first from the oldest one. I don't know if they were from Ejszyszki or from Vilna. And Teivele Blacha was the next, the third one. He start to yell and to scream so high. While he was screaming and while he was yelling, they hit him harder. They lifted him out of the stool. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they just put him again on the wall. Now I am on the line. They put me-- And he didn't stop to scream or yell and to cry. While he was staying on the wall they didn't touch the other two. But they were-- they got him-- he got them nervous while he was not stopping from crying or from yelling. They came to the wall and they beat him. When they started to beat me, I didn't cry, I didn't yell. I took the pain. I kept it to myself. And they start to wonder why I'm not crying, why I'm not yelling. So one guy from the Germans-- like I was laying with the face to them-- he sit down on the knee and kneels down, and he's looking me in the eyes, my reaction, why I am not crying, why I'm not-- I can take the pain. I'm not yelling. I'm not crying.

speaker: interviewee
text: He looks at me. I look at him, with no madness. I see if we have-- the-- with irony. Say what, what did you want from a little boy? What kind of enjoyment you're getting out-- you beating me? Is that your purpose to winning the war? Like-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, they got rid of me. They put me on the wall. And they start again to take the first one. Twice, everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: Meanwhile, came in another group of Jews they catch with another three Germans. So they have new victims, and they start to get rid of us. So we got our portion already. They tell us to rest ourselves. In the meanwhile, in the pause from us to the second group what's going to happen, they went to drink beer and to smoke cigars. In the window, the guy who came in with the new group, he start to, to scream and to curse. 'So much smoke here.' They used to smoke cigars. So he opens up the window to let the smoke out. And he went back to, to the new group he brought in. I didn't see how many he-- or how much he brought in, but they were standing outside in the second room. When the rest undressed and they told us to dressed up, I didn't dressed up. When I found the seconds, the minutes while they were dressing and changing the guards-- they went out to drink beer in the second room-- and he opened up the window to, to get out the smoke-- and I take a jump through the window. And I don't know where I'm going to jump. I don't know the circumstances of the building. I just remember I was about 10, 12 steps up. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I fell down, and it was a soft [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] ground. It was like-- and it was also in, in, in September. It was after a rain. You know, it was the ground-- this was a garden there. After me, Teivele, Teivele Blacha jumped. And he got to start yell and, and screaming. He broke-- he broke a leg while he was jumping. And they wouldn't know another two minutes that we disappeared if he would be successful to jump like me. I jump, and my foot slide in up till-- to the knee. And I sit down on my-- on my behind so nicely, like, like the rules and regulations from, from, from, from from exercise. When I pull out my foot back from the peat, and I start to run. I ran, I'm telling you, maybe 100 kilometers an hour. Where I got the strength, where I got the power, I don't know. But he start to yell and scream because he broke the leg. He didn't fall good. So they went out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you imagine after-- excuse me for my expression-- for a lousy Jewish kid, to put such an effort-- they light up-- they're having searchlights to put up in the air. They shoot up maybe about 10, 12 lights to light up the area to look for me, to find for me, because he was laying down there. He couldn't move because he broke his leg. That's what kind of effort-- oh, maybe about a dozen Germans running. They didn't find my direction where I was because it was dark at night. And I didn't know where I'm running, I'm just running. [SIGHS] What can I tell? I ran till the morning, with no stop, I did maybe about 15 kilometers, running. And the rain, it was dripping a little, cold rain. Not heavy rain, but a cold, small rain. And that makes me alive, fresh, Although my body-- my body was all colors. It was a color-- you couldn't find a normal color of my body except green, blue, red, from the beating up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I couldn't sit down to do my need. There was one piece of my foot. Everything was like a piece of wood if I was touching myself, so sore was my skin. When I got in-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I ran a lot of miles, a lot of miles till I got in, in a small wooden [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] bush, forest. Very low bushes. But I find a shelter there. And it was-- start to be very [INAUDIBLE]. The peat is so wet--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a shelter did you find?

speaker: interviewee
text: In between the bushes. And it was so wet, if I step in, I step in till my knees. And then I start to find-- till it came daylight, then I saw how to work over the-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] over the hills, not to step into the water. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Day came. I got a rest. I knew they wouldn't find me, because no human soul can get in, in this-- in this kind of bushes. It was-- already in the morning was a little frost in the water, but when you-- not strong enough to hold you. So while you're stepping in, you're stepping in up to here. And the water was so green. But it-- And I find a shelter there. I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I been there till the evening, but walking around to find myself the directions. I don't know where I am. I don't know what's my next step, where to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have any clothes at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. I left my clothes with the Germans, again, the second time. Because if I would be busy to put my clothes on, I-- it would be too late to run. So I figured let's use the opportunity and to run off, because I saw what they were doing-- the first group of the Germans and the second group of the Germans. Meanwhile it was like a little hole in my time to use that. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there, and you know, I lost my conscious a little. I didn't know what day is that. Is that Sunday, or Saturday? Is that Sunday, or Monday, or Tuesday? And then I gave up. I couldn't find myself-- what day is that, and how long passed by since the execution or the massacre when they catch me, when they grabbed me, when I'm running away? And I fall asleep. I fall asleep. While I'm sitting on a little high hill on the peat-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] but I feel pain in my whole body.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and it comes after the sun kind of start to set down. I start to go on the other side from where I go in. I don't know where I'm going. Eventually, I got out of the peat, and I saw a little building, like I show you on the picture. That's usually, in this kind of buildings, they are doing the cotton. The peasants, they are warming, they're eating, they're putting on heat there. They're putting the, the, the cotton what's growing. And it became cotton from the straw. That's usually-- that's the purpose of this kind of building. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I came in, into the buildings. And there was also a, a little man-made canal with water, for fish probably. I got in in this building. I start to feel a little warmer, because I start to shaking from cold to hot, [INAUDIBLE] wet. I dry off myself, laid over there. And in the-- and I fall asleep over there. But I lost my sense of directions. I lost my sense of conscious. [SIGHS] I didn't know where I am. But I was looking instinctively for shelter, and to some-- to dress myself on me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I, I, I slept a while. And then I saw stack with hay. You know, the farmers, they are cutting the grasses in this kind of peat. And they're concentrating it in big stacks. When it's winter, it's freezing. When it's snow, they're coming in, and they're taking the hay into the barracks. But summertime, it's impossible to do it, so they are cutting it. And I saw this stack of hay just before daylight comes on. I dig in myself in such a stack of hay. It was close, about 100 meter off from this place where I was-- from this building. When I dig in into this stack of hay, and it was-- the hay was dry and it was like, like needles to my body. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But I adjust myself to them. They was sticking me, to my-- my body was so painful. But I find myself. When I fall asleep in this hay-- I-- 50 years-- I tried to think how long I was sleeping there.

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- I figure-- I start to figure out, Sunday night I came in. Sunday night. When did I wake up? I don't know. If-- was it a week, or three days, or four days, or two weeks?

speaker: interviewee
text: I slept. I sleep there like with opium, like, like, like, like, like I took some drugs. And that's what maybe saved my life. Body got healed a little. I came back to myself, even I lost my conscious. But I came back to myself. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then, after I wake up, I start to slide out from the stack of hay outside. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I heard before, while I was in the stack of hay, the noise of birds around the stack of hay. But I never figure-- I never could figure that they are after me, that they are smelling me. But of course, its nature. That's their-- that's their land. They're looking after whatever-- food. And as I'm sliding out from this stack of  hay-- it was the highest part, three feet high from the bottom, from the ground-- they surround me, and they start to bite me on the feet, on the legs, wherever they could s-- they could. And I start to chase them away from myself. I said, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to pause here to change the tape.
